planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun; Nagami kumquat is the most widely grown and most readily available kumquat, the classic oval-fruited type with sweet edible skin and intensely tart flesh that are eaten whole.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil in full sun; kumquats are more cold-hardy than most citrus and can tolerate temperatures briefly to about 18–22°F when mature. Nagami kumquat is the most widely grown kumquat worldwide, producing oval (1–1.5 inch long), bright orange fruits with a sweet, aromatic edible peel and intensely sour-tart flesh and juice — the unique flavor is experienced by eating the entire fruit in one or two bites, allowing the sweet peel flavor to intermingle with the tart interior. The contrast of sweet and sour in a tiny package is delightfully surprising and different from any other citrus. Nagami fruits from November through January and holds on the tree for 3–4 months; the laden branches are extraordinarily ornamental and the trees make outstanding large container specimens that can be moved indoors in zones north of 8 for winter protection. Self-fruitful; no pollinator needed.
sources: University of California Riverside Citrus Variety Collection

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile soil in full sun; Meiwa is generally considered the finest-flavored kumquat for fresh eating, with thick, unusually sweet skin and sweeter interior flesh than Nagami, making it the most pleasant to eat whole without the intense pucker of standard kumquats.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Meiwa is self-fruitful and does not require a pollinator. Meiwa kumquat (also spelled Miehua or called 'Sweet Kumquat') is a round-fruited species hybrid distinct from the elongated Nagami, with notably thicker, sweeter edible peel and somewhat less acidic interior flesh than Nagami — making it the most approachable and pleasant kumquat for direct, whole-fruit eating without the sharp pucker of standard types. The round to slightly oval, golden-orange fruits (approximately 1 inch in diameter) ripen November through February and hold well on the tree. Meiwa trees are somewhat more compact than Nagami and are among the most cold-hardy citrus available, tolerating brief temperatures to 16–18°F. An outstanding ornamental container citrus for temperate climates and an excellent Gulf Coast landscape tree.
sources: University of California Riverside Citrus Variety Collection

planting.note: Plant container-grown grafted trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun; yuzu is among the most cold-hardy citrus species, tolerating temperatures to about 14–17°F when established and making it viable in zone 8 and some protected zone 7 sites where most citrus would be killed.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil in full sun; yuzu is self-fruitful and produces better with cross-pollination, though single-tree production is adequate. Yuzu (Citrus junos) is a Japanese and Chinese citrus species of great antiquity, prized throughout East Asian cuisine — particularly in Japanese cuisine — for its extraordinary aromatic zest and juice that has a flavor profile unlike any other citrus: intensely fragrant, complex, floral-tart with notes of grapefruit, mandarin, and pine. The rough, bumpy, medium-sized (2–3 inch diameter) yellow fruits are rarely eaten whole but their zest and juice are foundational in ponzu sauce, yuzu kosho, miso soup, hot pots (nabe), cakes, and flavoring sake. Yuzu trees are extremely thorny (the thorns can be 3–4 inches long) and slow to begin fruiting (5–8 years from seed; 3–5 years from grafted trees) but among the most cold-hardy of all citrus, surviving to 14–17°F. Now widely available as grafted plants and grown by American chefs and home gardeners.
sources: University of California Riverside Citrus Variety Collection

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile soil in full sun; Calamansi is the most widely used citrus in Philippine and Southeast Asian cooking, with tiny, round, intensely aromatic fruits used as a souring agent in virtually every aspect of Filipino cuisine — a must-have for Filipino cooks.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil in full sun; calamansi is self-fruitful and produces small, round fruits year-round in warm climates. Citrofortunella microcarpa (calamansi, calamondin, Philippine lime) is a kumquat-tangerine hybrid that is the most important souring citrus in Filipino cuisine and widely used in Southeast Asian cooking generally; the tiny (1–1.5 inch), round, orange-rinded fruits with intensely tart, aromatic green to orange juice are used in Philippine cooking the way lemons and limes are used in Western cuisine — squeezed over virtually every savory dish, mixed with soy sauce as the ubiquitous sawsawan dipping sauce, squeezed over grilled meats, noodles, soups, and rice. The distinctive, intensely floral-tart flavor is quite different from standard lime or lemon. Bears fruit almost continuously in warm climates; highly ornamental with glossy foliage. Outstanding as a large container citrus that can be moved indoors in colder zones.
sources: Philippine Council for Agriculture and Fisheries

planting.note: Plant container-grown grafted trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun; Sudachi is the signature souring citrus of Tokushima Prefecture, Japan — smaller than yuzu, used exclusively as a juice and zest ingredient, and one of the most aromatic and distinctively flavored of all Japanese citrus with an intense, complex fragrance unlike any other.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil in full sun; Sudachi is self-fruitful and produces tiny, green fruits that are harvested before they ripen to yellow for the most aromatic juice. Sudachi (Citrus sudachi) is a small, intensely aromatic souring citrus virtually unknown outside Japan but recently gaining attention in American culinary circles for its exceptional flavor; native and endemic to Tokushima Prefecture on Shikoku island (which produces approximately 98% of Japan's sudachi), the small (1–1.5 inch diameter), round, green fruits are always used before they ripen to yellow when their intense, fragrant juice and zest are at peak. The flavor is tart, fragrant, and floral with a complexity and intensity that surpasses lime or lemon — used by Japanese chefs exclusively for its aromatic quality as a garnish squeeze over grilled fish, mushrooms, soba noodles, and in ponzu sauce alternatives. Virtually unavailable commercially outside Japan; growing your own is the only way to access fresh sudachi in the US.
sources: University of California Riverside Citrus Variety Collection
